text: The surgeon demonstrates trocar placements for a laparoscopic splenectomy in a patient with splenomegaly, including a 5mm trocar in the epigastric region, two along the left costal margin, and a 12mm trocar superior and lateral to the umbilicus. The 12mm site is extended for specimen extraction. A clip is placed near the spleen, and blunt dissection is performed using a right angle instrument to prepare for energy device use. Dense adhesions between the stomach fundus and spleen, as well as between the spleen and diaphragm, are noted. Large short gastric vessels are clipped and cut with an energy device to prevent post-operative bleeding, ensuring no thermal injury to the stomach fundus.

The surgeon has mobilized the stomach and fundus, and after taking down the short gastric vessels, enters the lesser sac. The pancreas is visible with the pulsating splenic artery above it. The splenic artery is dissected and clipped to prevent bleeding during hilum dissection. A locking clip is applied, and the artery is divided using a stapling device with a one millimeter closed staple height. The splenocolic ligament is exposed and divided using an energy device, with caution taken to avoid damaging lower pole vessels due to the spleen's segmental blood supply.

The surgeon carefully surrounds vessels with an energy device to prevent incomplete cutting and potential bleeding, maintaining a distance of one to two centimeters from the spleen. The dissection proceeds cautiously to avoid injury to the tail of the pancreas, which may be near the splenic hilum. The splenorhinal ligament is being dissected as the spleen is elevated towards the abdominal wall. The splenic artery, vein, and hilum are visible, with no pancreatic tail observed in this region.

The surgeon attempts to dissect across the splenic hilum to fire a single load across all vessels but encounters difficulty in safely accessing the vessels behind the hilum. A large right angle instrument is used to try and reach the vessels, but the approach is abandoned. The surgeon then individually dissects the splenic artery and veins within the hilum. A branch of the splenic artery is clipped and divided with a ligature device, while another pulsating arterial branch is clipped proximally and distally before being divided with an energy device. The splenic vein is stapled and transected using a stapler with a one millimeter closed staple height, ensuring complete coverage across the vessels. The area is inspected and confirmed to be hemostatic.

The surgeon uses an energy device to dissect and remove the front splenic ligament, carefully manipulating the large spleen adhered to the diaphragm. A centimeter ridge of tissue is left on the spleen to aid in grasping without tearing the capsule. The remaining portion of the splenorhinal ligament is visualized and taken with the energy device, ensuring no injury to underlying structures. After mobilizing the spleen and removing all attachments and blood supply, the surgical field is irrigated to clear blood and inspected for hemostasis.
